speaker: interviewer
text: --two. Today is April 18, 1995. Survivor Dolf Ringel. Interviewer Bonnie [? Dwar. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summer of 1940, I became a member of a group of young Dutch Jewish people at the sport club of [? Macaby ?]. Some were friends of [? Dottie's. ?] And she introduced me to them. And a group of approximately eight young people-- young women and-- and young men-- had formed. And we became close friends. And in spite of the circumstances, and the Nazi occupation of Holland, we had a relatively [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] good and pleasant time in Holland in those days. In fact, for me it was one of the most happiest, the most happiest days of my life there. Because I had a girlfriend. And I knew that one day I-- I would get married to her. And very good friends. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 19-- when 1941 came, there were already some restrictions. Jews had to be-- there was a curfew on for Jews. We all had to be in at midnight. Everything was black. There was a blackout in every occupied country of Europe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in '41 when the Nazi decrees started, life became more difficult for us. We were not allowed to go to public parks, to cinemas, to theaters. Anything where-- what-- where general public was admitted, Jews were excluded. So we were driven to be more together, to-- to visit each other. We were still allowed to have sports activity-- in this case tennis-- only in a Jewish club, to which I belonged. In February 1941 came the first open attack against Jews in Amsterdam for-- for some fictitious reason. Young men between the ages of 18 and 35 were suddenly round-- rounded up in Amsterdam on February 1941. They were beaten and thrown into a truck-- in trucks-- by the SS and taken away. The Dutch gentile population saw that, and they rebelled against it, and called out-- the workmen of Amsterdam called out a strike, which developed into a general strike in Amsterdam. The Germans took hostages-- i don't know how many, but it must have been more than 25-- and executed them-- gentiles. And that broke the backbone of the strike. But as it is called in Dutch, [NON-ENGLISH]-- the February Strike-- lives in the memory of Holland, because it was the first time of violent reaction against the German oppressor. Those 400 young Jewish men were all sent to the concentration camp of Mauthausen. And not one survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In June, while we were playing tennis, [? Dottie ?] was sick at home. I got the telephone call through the-- to the tennis courts. It was [NON-ENGLISH], that's the Dutch abbreviation-- [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH]. Said get-- get away with Max. Max was another friend of ours. Get away with him. They are out in the street to-- and they are catching young men again. We jumped on our bicycles, Max and I. And we left the tennis courts, and saw already our respective mothers coming to get us. They also had heard the rumors that round-ups were again busy in Amsterdam. They brought us clothes. We went into the bushes, changed from the tennis shorts, et cetera, shoes, into regular clothes, jumped on our bicycles, and left for a-- it's not exactly a suburb. It's about a place 15 to 20 miles southeast of Amsterdam where Max knew a gentile-- very nice gentile family-- who had said to him once if there is any danger, you can come to my house at any time. We had the choice either to pedal along the river Amstel-- the river of Amsterdam-- or to cross a bridge, which was the shorter way. For some unknown reason, which I cannot explain, we decided to-- to bicycle along the river Amstel only to hear later on that on both sides of the bridge SS men were standing and arresting any young guy who looked Jewish. We made it to that place, and stayed there for a week, until we heard that everything had quieted down. We were still in those days under the totally wrong impression that all the anti-Jewish measures of the Nazis were meant against young men-- young Jewish men-- whom they may consider as soldiers, let's say-- enemy soldiers; and that women and children and the elderly would be allowed to live peacefully. I must admit I lived in a dream world still. I imagined things. I should have known better. But when a couple of months after that round-up, the parents-- parents of our friends, most of friends were caught in those days at that time. They all were going to play tennis. And on the way to the tennis courts in those streets they were all caught. 90% of them were caught. Two months later, postcards arrived to the parents that their son had died of pneumonia, of typhoid fever, of any kind of imaginary illness, and that they should send 30 marks for the ashes. That opened my eyes. Said they are-- they are out to murder us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Jewish Council-- the so-called Jewish Council-- was founded in the year 1941, who had to represent all Jews in Holland, and was, let's say, an in-between between the German occupying authorities and the Jew-- Jewish population in Holland. Two prominent members of the Jewish community in Amsterdam-- one, the owner of the largest diamond factory, and a former university professor-- became the heads of this [INAUDIBLE], the Jewish Council who handled all Jewish affairs. This was done by the Germans to avoid the public press or the radio to announce all these anti-Jewish measures that were now followed one after the other constantly, so that it should be done quietly-- almost secretly, not to enrage the Dutch non-Jewish population. And so we were-- we were informed that we had to give up our bicycles. We were informed that we had to give up our radios. We had to give up our business, our possessions. We were allowed only a certain amount of money to withdraw from-- from a bank which was now under German control. And so we-- we sputtered along during that year 1941. In June 1941, Germany attacked Russia. And now Max and I, Max also was a former schoolmate of my brothers in the same school when I met him in Amsterdam. We decided that it might be better not to live at home anymore. I changed officially my address from where I lived to a totally different address-- a gentile lady whom I knew who was willing to say that I lived there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The idea was that if anyone came asking for me, that my parents could say that they don't know where I am. I moved out. And my address was such and such now. And they haven't seen me in a week or so. I wanted to protect my parents. And slowly but surely a plan ripened in Max and my mind to try to escape from Holland. The shortest escape route was through Switzerland. And we had heard rumors. And a second cousin of mine who lived in Holland, he had informed me of that possibility to go-- to get to Antwerp in Belgium, and that there there where so-called [NON-ENGLISH]-- a French word for a smuggler-- who could take people to non-occupied France. You may know that after France was defeated, Hitler created an occupied zone which was the western part of France. And the eastern part of France was the non-occupied or Vichy France zone. That was on the French government. Of course, the Germans still had their-- their influence there. But it was still-- had still a certain extent of freedom for escapees. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I told [? Dottie ?] of my plan, she said, don't do it. You think that only young men are in danger. I have the feeling that it will be every single one-- men, women, children, anyone. Well, I tried to convince her that all measures that had been taken so far were mainly against young men. Our parents, who hadn't like everyone else, hadn't recognized yet the deadly danger that all of us were in, they urged us-- me, of course, and Max-- his parents-- to leave. Before I left in November '41 I decided to go to [? Dottie's ?] father and ask for her hand, the old-fashioned way. And I knew there would be-- there would be no problem, because I noticed that they liked me very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he spoke to me and said, what would you do-- what will you do after the war, when the war ends? So I explained to him what I was going back to business, and that I intended to [INAUDIBLE] and [? that. ?] He was satisfied.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had a little party. That means my [? Dottie's ?] family, her parents and two brothers, and my parents, and [? Dottie's ?] aunt. That was the party. And my father-in-law-- after we were all-- we were congratulated-- took out one of his precious Dutch cigars, and handed me one. Then we went and bought-- we had gold someplace still hidden. We bought gold wedding rings, which I still have. And we made pictures which we still have, our engagement pictures. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the first few days of I think it may have been December 3rd or 4th--

speaker: interviewee
text: --1941, Mark-- Max-- Max and I left. We traveled to a small village in the southeastern part of Holland, where the Dutch-- where the Belgian border runs through the middle of the village. [CLEARING THROAT] Excuse me. On one side was Holland. The other side was Belgium. We took the train through that part, and a bus; entered a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a pub I would call it, and ordered a beer. And we had been instructed beforehand that the backyard-- you could get-- get out the rear door. There is a backyard. You walk along those backyards for maybe 50 yards, and you are in Belgium. Then the first little path that you find, you make a left turn, and you are on the main street. And there is a bus that takes you to Antwerp. That's exactly what we did. We got to Antwerp by the street car. No control. Everything went fine. And went to an apartment of my mother's aunt, an old lady who lived with her spinster daughter in Antwerp. The next day we went to a Jewish contact that was given to us in Amsterdam by my second cousin, and said to him that we had heard that he can arrange for convoy to Switzerland via Lyon. Lyon is in southwestern-- southeastern part of France, and was in the non-occupied zone of France. He said, well, a convoy has just left. Convoy consisted of maybe four or five people taken by a smuggler usually by train and buses to-- to Lyon. He said, one convoy has just left. Come back next Monday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three or four days later, when we came back on Monday morning, he was there. And he said, didn't you read the papers? We said what papers? No, we didn't read newspapers. So then you haven't heard yet? I said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, and America is now at war with Japan, and Germany declared war on-- on United States. And no convoy is going for the foreseeable future. I'm sorry. I can't help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were dumbfounded. Went back to my aunt and started deliberating. And I said to Max, Max, do you think what I'm thinking? He said, yes. We go back. I said, exactly. What are we going to do here? Then we are better off in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back the same way we came. When [? Dottie ?] saw me back, she almost fainted. She didn't believe she would ever see me again. Her premonitions were much, much better than mine although I had seen the growing power of Nazism in Germany. Her premonitions as a-- as a woman usually has-- were better. 1942 came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And early in 1942 came the yellow star the Jews had to wear. We started preparing. We don't-- we didn't live at home anymore. We had an address with a gentile woman in the western part of Amsterdam, which is for the work-- workmen part of Amsterdam-- lower-- lower income class. We entered a room there and slept there. We only came out for a few hours to visit either my parents or his parents, and-- and [? Dottie. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the noose became tighter and tighter. And on the 5th of July 1942, I was just coming home, going back to my apartment from [? Dottie's. ?] I saw the mailman going up the steps to our apartment. And he said where does the Ringel family live? I said I am one of them. He handed me a card, a special delivery card.

speaker: interviewee
text: That special delivery card said, it came from the German authorities. You are herewith ordered to appear on I believe it was July 10th at 8:00 PM in the evening at the central station of Amsterdam to be sent to a labor camp in the east-- or Germany. I forgot. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Max had established before that time a relationship with a Dutch gentleman, a stockbroker, a wealthy gentleman who had a house in the center of Amsterdam. And the first floor was the-- his business-- stockbroker business with several employees. The second floor he had the-- he was the general secretary of the church-- the schools of the reform church in the Netherlands. And there were several employees on the second floor. On the third floor were his living quarters. And then there was an attic. And that's when I got my card. Max got his card on the same day. We both knew that's when we are going to go to Mr. Alexander Wellensiek. That was the name of this gentleman belonging to one of the old Amsterdam patrician families. Incidentally this building where we now went to and were hiding out in the attic was only a few blocks away from the building where Anne Frank lived, also in the center of Amsterdam, and in a similar house in a similar attic. Very similar, both things, both situations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were instructed that you in daytime we had to take off our shoes and walk only in stocking feet. There was enough food. He didn't take any money for food. He did it out of patriotism and love of humanity. He was a fervent patriot-- Dutch patriot-- and hated the German invaders with a passion. And for him there was no difference between a Protestant, a Catholic, a Jew, and a Muslim. All human beings were alike, as long as they kept the human ethical values. When I ask him, but you are risking your life for us, he said, I know that. But if I can save two people from the German madness, that is my reward. We didn't know at that time that Lex, as we called him-- or Uncle Lex-- was deeply involved in the Dutch underground. We didn't know it yet. Only my parents, Max's parents, my in-laws, and [? Dottie ?] knew where we were. No one else. Because you did not give these addresses to anyone in order not to endanger our benefactor, not to endanger our own lives. Shortly, maybe a couple of weeks after, we had found shelter there, [? Dottie ?] had to leave her house, too, when a search by the SS from house to house in the street where we lived almost caught her and her younger brother. They escaped by hiding out, she may have [? told ?] it, by hiding out in the house. So she escaped to the address that I had also, that I myself had prepared beforehand possibly for my parents should the need arise. Now we started corresponding with each other by using the business envelopes of my-- our benefactor. And her-- the gentleman where she-- at the house where she was hiding out, he used his envelopes. And that was the way how we could correspond with each other. In the evening we listened to the British radio. And it became quite clear to us-- to all three of us-- that this would be a long, long war. Very long. And increasingly, Max and I were talking about our hiding out there. We noticed the goings-on in the evening, when we were allowed to come down to the private quarters of our friend, that underground figures came. A newspaper, underground newspaper which still exists today, was founded in that house. And all kind of measures were deliberated about in his apartment. Money was collected.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Max and I became aware of the grave danger that we were in-- but especially so our benefactor. Because if at any time someone, let's say, surprised us by-- by coming up unannounced, which fortunately never happened, they would see two-- two men hiding out. The life of our-- our friend wouldn't be worth a cent. He would be lost. And so were we. So we started thinking of escaping.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now in August, [? Dottie's ?] older brother-- older brother-- Eddie-- made an escape from Amsterdam and managed to reach Switzerland.
